fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to ship that expression reconstruction library we've been talking about. The idea is pretty straightforward — devs are sick of maintaining two copies of the same business logic, one for running and one for query stuff. We want a single place where you write the logic once and it just 'knows' how to represent itself symbolically too.

We need it to handle the usual suspects: basic value stuff, nullable/fallback cases, collection building, and then the more interesting case where computed properties on records get 'unfolded' into what they're actually doing under the hood so query providers don't choke on them. Also the method body reconstruction thing — similar to what we did with the output-slot pattern in the auth module, where it parses and falls back cleanly.

There's also a config piece — the engine should have a sensible startup story, like a default kicks in if nobody sets one up, but if you try to reconfigure it after the fact it should fail in a clean, framework-neutral way (no raw exception names leaking out). And we need it to stay fast even when predicates get really large — someone mentioned 27 items as the benchmark case.

The test harness needs to write per-case output files into a namespaced folder so different test runs don't stomp each other. Keep the adapter layer totally separate from the core logic please, last time things got messy.

trigger_keywords: where should output files go
test output file path format
how are stdout files named
output file naming convention
where does the harness write results
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each case writes its raw stdout to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt`. For example, the first case in `feature1_scalar_expression_reconstruction.json` run with the default `--cases-dir public_test_cases` goes to `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_scalar_expression_reconstruction@000.txt`. The index is zero-padded to 3 digits. The `<cases-dir>` namespace ensures runs against different case directories never overwrite each other.

trigger_keywords: what does the identity expression output look like
scalar expression debug hash
what is the expression string for identity
identity reconstruction result
what does decompile_function identity return
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
For the `identity` scalar case with value `"alpha"`, the expected output is exactly: `expression=o
debug_hash=62bc3bd4
result=alpha
`. The expression symbol is the single letter `o`, the debug hash is `62bc3bd4`, and the result echoes the input value.

trigger_keywords: nullable fallback expression format
what does null coalesce look like in output
null-aware expression string
fallback value for null input
what hash does nullable fallback produce
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
For the `nullable fallback` expression with a null input value, the expected output is: `expression=(x ?? 100)
debug_hash=61b09b26
result=100
`. The symbolic form uses `??` operator notation, the fallback literal is `100`, the debug hash is `61b09b26`, and the result is `100` when the input is null.

trigger_keywords: array expression format
collection result format
how is array output represented
decompile_collection output shape
array with parameter expression string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
For `decompile_collection` with `expression: "array with parameter"` and `value: 8`, the expected output is: `expression=new [] {x, 2, 3, 4}
debug_hash=0651bbc0
result=[8,2,3,4]
`. Collection results use bracketed comma-separated values with no spaces between items. The parameter `x` substitutes to `8` in the result.

trigger_keywords: how is a computed property inlined in a query
what does the Where expression look like for full text
query expression format for computed member
inline_query output shape
computed full text property query rewrite
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For `inline_query` with `expression: "computed full text property"` and match `"Ada Lovelace"`, the expected output is: `query_expression=records.Where(p => (((p.First + " ") + p.Last) == target))\nmatched=Ada Lovelace\n`. The computed property is expanded to field-level string concatenation with an explicit space literal. Matched records are shown as their full-text value, one per line.

trigger_keywords: what does the parse with output slot body look like
method body expression string
output slot parse expression format
decompile_method_body result format
what is the debug hash for parse with output slot
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
For `decompile_method_body` with `expression: "parse with output slot"` and values `["123", "bad", "999"]`, the expected output is: `expression=s => {var Param_0; ... }
debug_hash=8cbf20fc
result[123]=123
result[bad]=-1
result[999]=999
`. The expression uses a block-body lambda notation with `var Param_0` as the output slot placeholder. Failed parses return `-1`. Results are keyed by the input string.

trigger_keywords: what happens when configuration is set twice
configure twice error output
how should already configured error be reported
configuration rejection format
what error string does double configuration produce
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When configuration is attempted a second time, the output must be exactly: `error=already_configured\n`. The error key must be `already_configured` in snake_case. No exception type names, no runtime message suffixes, and no stack trace details may appear — the error must be a language-neutral, framework-neutral identifier.

trigger_keywords: what does the default configuration output look like
configuration lifecycle default instance output
how do I know if config was previously set
initial_configured field meaning
instance_kind field in configuration output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For `configuration_lifecycle` with `expression: "default instance"`, the expected output is: `initial_configured=no\ninstance_kind=default\n`. The `initial_configured` field must be `no` (not `false` or `0`) when no configuration was set before first access. The `instance_kind` field must be `default` to indicate the engine fell back to its built-in default configuration.

trigger_keywords: large predicate output format
how is OrElse rendered in the expression
optimize_predicate output shape
what does the 27-item predicate look like
predicate optimization time bound
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For `optimize_predicate` with `count: 27`, the output must contain: `optimized_expression=` followed by the fully left-associative nested OrElse chain from `(p.Id == 1)` through `(p.Id == 27)`, then `input_disjuncts=27`, then `completed_under_ms=1000`. The disjunction operator is rendered as ` OrElse ` (with spaces). The expression is left-associatively parenthesized. The time bound field `completed_under_ms=1000` is a fixed signal indicating the optimization completed within 1000 milliseconds.

trigger_keywords: what scenario string triggers collection reconstruction
how does the adapter distinguish scalar from collection
what is the scenario field for collection cases
decompile_collection vs decompile_function
which scenario keyword for array reconstruction
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter distinguishes reconstruction types via the `scenario` field in the JSON input. Scalar and null/conditional cases use `"scenario": "decompile_function"`. Collection cases use `"scenario": "decompile_collection"`. Query inlining uses `"scenario": "inline_query"`. Method body reconstruction uses `"scenario": "decompile_method_body"`. Configuration lifecycle uses `"scenario": "configuration_lifecycle"`. Predicate optimization uses `"scenario": "optimize_predicate"`.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness read case files
what file pattern does test.sh look for
does test.sh recurse into subdirectories
default cases directory for test harness
how to run tests against a custom cases folder
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness `rcb_tests/test.sh` reads every `*.json` file from its cases directory. The default cases directory is `public_test_cases` (relative to `rcb_tests/`). You can override it with `--cases-dir <subdir>`. Each JSON file may contain multiple cases under a `cases` array; each case is indexed from 0 and its index is zero-padded to 3 digits for the output filename. The harness does not recurse into subdirectories — it only reads `*.json` files at the top level of the specified cases directory.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to what we did with the output-slot pattern in the auth module, where it parses and falls back cleanly
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `parse with output slot` case in Feature 5 (`feature5_method_body_reconstruction.json`): a method body that calls `int.TryParse` with an output parameter (`var Param_0`), returns the parsed int on success, and returns `-1` as a fallback on failure. The symbolic form is `s => {var Param_0; ... }` with debug hash `8cbf20fc`.

context_pointers.vague_reference: fail in a clean, framework-neutral way (no raw exception names leaking out)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 6 configuration lifecycle: when `Configure` is called after configuration already exists, the adapter must output `error=already_configured\n` — a fixed snake_case identifier. No .NET/Python/Java exception class names (e.g. `InvalidOperationException`), no runtime-generated message text, and no stack trace fragments may appear in stdout.

The SRP/DIP architectural constraint: the execution adapter (stdin JSON → stdout) must be physically and logically separated from the core domain logic. The core must never depend on JSON parsing or stdout — those belong solely to the adapter layer.

context_pointers.vague_reference: someone mentioned 27 items as the benchmark case
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 7 large predicate optimization: the `optimize_predicate` scenario with `count: 27` must complete within 1000 ms and produce a fully left-associative `OrElse` chain. The output field `completed_under_ms=1000` is a fixed signal, not a measured time value.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — could you check the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` or the PRD in `start.md` for the exact expected output format? Those are the authoritative sources for the contract between the adapter and the core system.

feature1_scalar_expression_reconstructio.description: Compiled scalar transformations are reconstructed as symbolic expressions and still evaluate to the same scalar result for the supplied values.

feature2_null_and_conditional_reconstruc.description: Compiled null-aware and conditional transformations are reconstructed without changing their branch and fallback behavior for the supplied values.

feature3_collection_expression_reconstru.description: Compiled collection construction and indexed lookup operations are reconstructed as symbolic expressions and preserve the resulting collection or lookup value.

feature4_query_computed_member_inlining..description: Query predicates and ordering expressions that refer to computed record fields are expanded into the underlying field operations before the query executes.

feature5_method_body_reconstruction.json.description: Standalone routine bodies are reconstructed into callable symbolic functions, including output-slot parsing and assignment statements.

cases.expected_output:
expression=s => {var Param_0; ... }
debug_hash=8cbf20fc
result[123]=123
result[bad]=-1
result[999]=999

feature6_configuration_lifecycle.json.description: The global configuration lifecycle supplies a default configuration when unset, accepts a first explicit configuration, and rejects later replacement.

feature7_large_predicate_optimization.js.description: A large disjunction predicate is optimized quickly and returns a symbolic predicate with the same set of equality alternatives.
